metadata.title: Clonal Resistance Prediction — The Cells That Beat the Drug Were There Before
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E body text; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral
metadata.note: CLI video REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT · B05 CHANGE · B06 OUTPUT · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.

beats.narration_text: Hej, Liam. This is Liam, in for Bear. Clonal resistance — the cells that beat the drug were there before you started.

Every targeted cancer therapy creates resistance. The resistant cells weren't created by the drug — they were already present at diagnosis, too rare to detect. The drug selects for them. By the time progression is visible on imaging, the selection battle is already over. Can we detect and pre-empt it?
Research clonal evolution of EGFR-mutant NSCLC under targeted therapy: resistance mechanisms by frequency, approved therapy sequence, and ctDNA detection windows.

props.command: claude "Research clonal evolution of EGFR-mutant NSCLC under targeted therapy: map the resistance mechanisms by frequency (T790M, C797S, MET amplification, HER2 amplification, histologic transformation), the approved second and third-generation therapy sequence, and the point at which no approved therapy exists. For each resistance mechanism, identify whether ctDNA can detect it before imaging."

beats.narration_text: Python script building EGFR resistance decision tree with mutation frequencies and therapy options.

props.code:
NODES = [
    {"mutation": "T790M",       "freq_pct": 55, "next_therapy": "Osimertinib", "ctdna_lead_mo": 4},
    {"mutation": "MET amp",     "freq_pct": 15, "next_therapy": "None approved", "ctdna_lead_mo": 3},
    {"mutation": "HER2 amp",    "freq_pct":  5, "next_therapy": "None approved", "ctdna_lead_mo": 2},
    {"mutation": "C797S (post-osi)", "freq_pct": 20, "next_therapy": "Trial only", "ctdna_lead_mo": 5},
    {"mutation": "SCLC transform",   "freq_pct":  5, "next_therapy": "Platinum-etoposide", "ctdna_lead_mo": None},
]
for n in NODES:
    lead = f"{n['ctdna_lead_mo']}mo early" if n['ctdna_lead_mo'] else "not detectable"
    print(f"{n['mutation']:<22} {n['freq_pct']:>3}%  next: {n['next_therapy']}  ctDNA: {lead}")

beats.narration_text: Decision tree showing EGFR resistance cascade — T790M to osimertinib, C797S with no approved therapy, bypass mechanisms with no approved therapy.
Add the ctDNA detection window for each resistance node — how many months before imaging does a liquid biopsy typically detect the resistance clone?

props.command: claude "Add the ctDNA detection window for each resistance node: how many months before imaging does a ctDNA liquid biopsy typically detect the resistance clone?"

beats.narration_text: Timeline showing ctDNA rising 3 to 6 months before imaging progression — the intervention window.

The resistance clone is always there first. ctDNA gives us a 3 to 6 month window — but only if the test is ordered, interpreted correctly, and the next therapy is available. Three requirements, any one of which can fail.

Read Abbosh et al. 2017 in Nature — TRACERx tracking clonal dynamics with ctDNA in NSCLC. Count how many patients had ctDNA rise before clinical relapse and how many had the opposite. That ratio is the sensitivity you're actually buying.
Liam, in for Bear. Build it, then take it apart.
